title: Christ, the True Temple
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: The First Year of Jesus' Ministry

outline.title: Introduction: Three Familiar Passages We Miss
subpoints: Three passages in John 2-4 so familiar we stop seeing them: the temple cleansing (ch. 2), Nicodemus (ch. 3), the Samaritan woman (ch. 4)
Jesus cleansed the temple twice — his ministry is bookended by these events; this first cleansing is unique to John
The main point is not anger management, revolutionary zeal, or whip-making — it is Christ as the place where the nations meet God to worship

outline.title: 1. Why Jesus Was There: Fulfilling All Righteousness
subpoints: Every Jewish male was required to come to Jerusalem for Passover (Exodus 23); Jesus fulfilled every requirement of the law perfectly — his obedience becomes ours at the moment of faith
This is the first of three Passovers in John — probably AD 28, about 6 months after his baptism
Ultimately, Jesus was in Jerusalem to save his people; everything he does is purposeful — he does nothing 'just because'

outline.title: 2. Why Jesus Cleared the Court: Room for the Nations
subpoints: The animals were sold and money was changed in the Court of the Gentiles — the only area of the temple where God-fearing non-Jews had access to worship
The vendors provided real convenience (worshipers arriving from afar didn't need to bring animals) and the money changers enabled payment of the required temple tax
But convenience is not the standard for worship — faithfulness to what God commands is; the noise and stench of commerce drove out the solemnity and room for the nations
Malachi 3:1,3: the Lord himself would suddenly come to his temple as a refiner and purifier — Jesus entered as the fulfillment of that prophecy

outline.title: 3. The Sign: Christ as the True Temple
subpoints: Religious leaders demanded a sign of authority — Jesus gave one they didn't understand: 'Destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up'
He used the word for the inner sanctuary (holy place + holy of holies) — the place where God dwells and sacrifices are offered — pointing to his own body
The resurrection is the definitive sign: Jesus consistently pointed to it in response to sign-seekers (Matthew 12:39-40 — sign of Jonah)
John 1:14: the Word 'tabernacled' among us (eskenosen = pitched his tent); Revelation 21:22: 'I saw no temple in it, for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple'

outline.title: Application: Where Do You Meet God?
subpoints: Christ replaces the temple in function — he is the place where man meets God, where atonement is made, where forgiveness is found
No pilgrimage required, no building, no animal sacrifice — faith in the Lord Jesus Christ is the only access to God
The disciples only understood after the resurrection; we too live in ongoing unbelief — 'Lord, I believe; help my unbelief'

summary: Continuing the series on the first year of Jesus' public ministry, Pastor Tito Lyro preaches on John 2:13-22 — the first cleansing of the temple. The main point is not anger management or revolutionary zeal but worship: Jesus came as the fulfillment of Malachi 3:1,3 (the Lord suddenly coming to his temple) and cleared the Court of the Gentiles — the only place non-Jews could worship — because he came to save the nations. The sign Jesus offered in response to the authorities' challenge was the resurrection: 'Destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up,' referring to his own body. Christ replaces the temple in function as the place where man meets God, where atonement is made. John 1:14 (the Word 'tabernacled' among us) and Revelation 21:22 (the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple) frame the theological claim: Jesus is the eternal temple of God.
transcript:
## Introduction: Familiar Passages We Miss

We are reading from the Gospel of John chapter 2, verses 13 through 22, as we continue our series on the first year of Jesus' public ministry.

There are some passages in the Bible so familiar to us that we read them without thinking much about them. As we continue through the first year of Jesus' ministry — which John covers in chapters 1-4 — we will encounter at least three such passages: Jesus' interaction with Nicodemus in chapter 3, his conversation with the Samaritan woman in chapter 4, and the one we just read: the cleansing of the temple in chapter 2.

One thing we may not realize is that Jesus cleansed the temple twice. His three-year public ministry is bookended by this action. Here in John 2 is the first cleansing. The second — the one recorded in Matthew, Mark, and Luke — happens on Sunday night or Monday morning of the week he was crucified. The significance of the bookending should not escape us.

I have heard many sermons on this passage. Some say it is about expressing anger constructively. Others say it is about anger management — Jesus took time to make a whip, so he was managing his anger. Others say it is about being a revolutionary. None of these is the main point. This passage is primarily about Christ being the place where the nations meet God to worship him.

## 1. Why Jesus Was There

Verse 13: *Now the Passover of the Jews was at hand, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.*

Why was Jesus in Jerusalem? For the same reason every Jewish male in Palestine was in Jerusalem: the law required it. Exodus 23 stipulates three annual feasts at which every male must appear in Jerusalem — Passover, Pentecost, and Tabernacles. Jesus kept every requirement of the law perfectly — every commandment, every feast, every obligation — because you and I cannot. At the moment we place our faith in Christ, his perfect obedience becomes ours.

This is the first of three Passovers in John — probably AD 28, about six months after Jesus' baptism. John organizes his account around these feasts to help us follow the chronology of Jesus' life.

Ultimately, Jesus was in Jerusalem to save his people. Everything he does is purposeful and intentional. He is not merely checking a liturgical obligation. He is in Jerusalem that Passover because that was his mission.

## 2. Why He Cleared the Court

Verses 14-15: *And he found in the temple those who sold oxen and sheep and doves, and the money-changers doing business. When he had made a whip of cords, he drove them all out of the temple, with the sheep and the oxen, and poured out the changers' money and overturned the tables.*

The animal sellers were providing genuine convenience. If you had traveled from far away, bringing a sacrificial animal with you was difficult. The vendors set up stalls in the temple precincts so worshipers could purchase animals on arrival. The money changers were there to exchange the various currencies of the Roman Empire into the Tyrian coin — the only currency accepted for the temple tax, because the Tyrians had developed a method for purifying silver to near 100% purity.

But the vendors had set up in the Court of the Gentiles — the only area of the temple where God-fearing non-Jews had access to worship God. This was the only place in the temple that the nations could come. Jesus had come to save the nations. And the court of the nations was filled with animals, noise, stench, and commerce.

Jesus arrived at the temple, not as just another worshiper, but as the one Malachi 3:1,3 predicted — *the Lord whom you seek will suddenly come to his temple*. He came as the refiner: *He will sit as a refiner and purifier of silver; he will purify the sons of Levi.* The cleansing of the temple is a symbolic fulfillment of that prophecy. Jesus cleared room for the nations by clearing the court of the nations.

His disciples eventually remembered what was written in Psalm 69:9: *Zeal for your house has consumed me.* D.A. Carson notes: evangelism exists because worship doesn't. Jesus went to the cross to secure for himself worshipers from every nation. The zeal that consumed him all the way to the cross was the proper worship of God by all the nations.

The whip? Jesus was driving large numbers of animals out of the court. A whip is exactly what you use for that. The whip is not the point.

The standard for worship is not convenience but faithfulness to what God commands. Convenience caused people to bring forbidden things into the worship of God — just as the sons of Aaron brought strange fire, and those who took the Lord's Supper in an unworthy manner. God requires faithfulness, not innovation.

## 3. The Sign: Christ as the True Temple

Verses 18-22: *The Jews asked him, 'What sign do you show us, since you do these things?' Jesus answered, 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.' The Jews said, 'It has taken forty-six years to build this temple, and will you raise it up in three days?' But he was speaking about the temple of his body.*

The authorities came to Jesus rightly: who authorized you to do this? Give us a sign. Jesus gave them one — but not the kind they expected. He promised a sign they would themselves participate in providing: *You destroy this temple, and I will raise it up in three days.*

The word Jesus used for *temple* here is not the word for the entire temple complex — it is the word for the inner sanctuary: the holy place and the holy of holies, the room where God dwells and sacrifices are offered. He is pointing to himself. He is the place where God meets man, where sacrifice is made, where forgiveness is found.

Jesus consistently pointed to the resurrection as the defining sign (Matthew 12:39-40 — the sign of Jonah: three days in the belly of the earth). The forty-six years of Herod's renovation project made their protest seem rational. But Jesus was speaking of his body.

John already hinted at this in chapter 1, verse 14: *The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.* The word *dwelt* is not the common Greek word for living; it means *pitched his tent* — the same word as the tabernacle. Jesus came to tabernacle among us as that one in whom we meet God.

And in Revelation 21:22, the same author: *I saw no temple in it, for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple.* The temple was a pointer. Christ is the destination.

In cleansing the temple and then declaring himself to be the true temple, Jesus is saying: this building was great, it taught you everything about forgiveness and access to God — but I am the ultimate reality to which it pointed. I am the mediator who brings man and God together. In me, the nations meet God. Not in a building, not through animal sacrifices, but through faith in me.

## Application

Verse 22: *When he had risen from the dead, his disciples remembered that he had said this to them, and they believed the Scripture and the word that Jesus had spoken.*

The disciples lived with Jesus for three years and still did not understand until the resurrection and the Spirit opened their eyes. Are we much different? We too live with ongoing unbelief — failing to believe that our salvation is completely accomplished in Christ, failing to believe that he is truly sovereign over our lives.

*Lord, I believe; help my unbelief.*

This passage is about worship. Is Jesus your temple — the place where you meet God, the sacrifice made for you, the mediator between you and the Father? Or are you still outside the temple looking in?

Father, thank you that Jesus is the true temple of God — that in him we find you, find forgiveness, find access. Strengthen our faith in him. In his name, amen.
